content.text: Purpose of the Benefits Realisation Review
A benefits realisation review is a structured quarterly meeting that answers one question:
are the GenAI programme's promised benefits actually materialising, and if not, what will we do about it?
It is not a celebration of successes or a project status update. Its output is either a confirmation that benefits are on track, or a set of course-correction actions with owners and deadlines.
The review is only valuable if it produces decisions, not just discussions. If a metric is RED and the meeting ends without a named action and a deadline, the review failed.

Many programmes launch, deploy, and then abandon the benefits register. The investment case promised savings or revenue; nobody ever checks whether those savings arrived. The quarterly review closes that gap and creates accountability that extends past the project close date.

attrs.alt: Benefits realisation review cycle: Collect actuals, Assess RAG vs baseline, Diagnose root causes, Decide course correction (Optimise / Remediate / Retire), loop back
attrs.title: Benefits Realisation Review Cycle

content.text: The four-phase cycle runs quarterly. The Collect and Assess phases are data-driven and should take no more than two hours of PM preparation. The Diagnose and Decide phases are the meeting itself: 60–90 minutes with the business owner, PM, and one technical lead. The output feeds into the next quarter's tracking.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional

class CourseCorrection(Enum):
    OPTIMISE   = "optimise"   # system performing, fine-tune to close the gap
    REMEDIATE  = "remediate"  # significant underperformance, structured intervention
    RETIRE     = "retire"     # benefit is unachievable; write it off or pivot scope
    ON_TRACK   = "on_track"   # no action needed

@dataclass
class MetricReviewOutcome:
    metric_id: str
    baseline_value: float
    target_value: float
    current_value: float
    progress_pct: float           # % of target gap closed
    rag: str                      # GREEN / AMBER / RED
    root_cause: str               # for AMBER/RED metrics
    course_correction: CourseCorrection
    action_owner: str
    action_deadline: date
    action_description: str

    def is_actionable(self) -> bool:
        return self.course_correction != CourseCorrection.ON_TRACK

@dataclass
class BenefitsRealisationReview:
    programme: str
    quarter: str
    review_date: date
    attendees: list[str]
    metric_outcomes: list[MetricReviewOutcome]
    next_review_date: date
    overall_verdict: str     # one sentence plain-language summary

    def actions(self) -> list[MetricReviewOutcome]:
        return [m for m in self.metric_outcomes if m.is_actionable()]

    def metrics_by_rag(self, rag: str) -> list[MetricReviewOutcome]:
        return [m for m in self.metric_outcomes if m.rag == rag]

    def formatted_action_list(self) -> str:
        lines = ["ACTIONS FROM BENEFITS REVIEW:"]
        for a in self.actions():
            lines.append(
                f"  [{a.course_correction.value.upper()}] {a.metric_id}: "
                f"{a.action_description} (owner: {a.action_owner}, by {a.action_deadline})"
            )
        return "\n".join(lines)

When a metric is AMBER or RED at the quarterly review, the diagnosis must identify the root cause before deciding the course correction. The four most common root causes for GenAI benefit shortfalls are:
What % of eligible users are using the system?
Does the model's output quality meet the use-case threshold?
Eval scores, user feedback, accuracy metrics
Optimise: fine-tune, add RAG, improve prompt
Did the surrounding process change to capture the benefit?
Remediate: redesign process with business owner
Was the business case assumption validated pre-launch?
Retire/pivot: re-scope or write off the benefit

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class DiagnosisCategory(Enum):
    LOW_ADOPTION          = "low_adoption"
    MODEL_QUALITY_GAP     = "model_quality_gap"
    PROCESS_NOT_REDESIGNED= "process_not_redesigned"
    UNREALISTIC_TARGET    = "unrealistic_target"
    CONFOUND              = "external_confound"
    UNKNOWN               = "unknown"

@dataclass
class BenefitDiagnosis:
    metric_id: str
    category: DiagnosisCategory
    evidence: str
    confidence: str           # "high", "medium", "low"
    recommended_correction: CourseCorrection
    rationale: str

def diagnose_benefit_shortfall(
    metric_id: str,
    adoption_rate: float,          # 0.0–1.0
    model_eval_score: float,       # 0.0–1.0
    process_redesign_complete: bool,
    target_was_validated: bool,
    active_confounds: list[str],
) -> BenefitDiagnosis:
    """
    Heuristic diagnosis. In practice, combine with qualitative interviews.
    Returns the most likely root cause and a recommended correction.
    """
    if adoption_rate < 0.3:
        return BenefitDiagnosis(
            metric_id=metric_id,
            category=DiagnosisCategory.LOW_ADOPTION,
            evidence=f"Adoption rate is {adoption_rate:.0%} (threshold: 30%)",
            confidence="high",
            recommended_correction=CourseCorrection.OPTIMISE,
            rationale="Benefits cannot accrue at < 30% adoption regardless of model quality",
        )
    if model_eval_score < 0.75:
        return BenefitDiagnosis(
            metric_id=metric_id,
            category=DiagnosisCategory.MODEL_QUALITY_GAP,
            evidence=f"Model eval score {model_eval_score:.0%} below 75% threshold",
            confidence="high",
            recommended_correction=CourseCorrection.OPTIMISE,
            rationale="Model accuracy insufficient for the use case",
        )
    if not process_redesign_complete:
        return BenefitDiagnosis(
            metric_id=metric_id,
            category=DiagnosisCategory.PROCESS_NOT_REDESIGNED,
            evidence="Workflow change management not completed",
            confidence="medium",
            recommended_correction=CourseCorrection.REMEDIATE,
            rationale="GenAI tool available but operating process unchanged; benefits locked out",
        )
    if not target_was_validated:
        return BenefitDiagnosis(
            metric_id=metric_id,
            category=DiagnosisCategory.UNREALISTIC_TARGET,
            evidence="Business case assumption not validated pre-launch",
            confidence="medium",
            recommended_correction=CourseCorrection.RETIRE,
            rationale="Target may have been aspirational rather than evidence-based",
        )
    if active_confounds:
        return BenefitDiagnosis(
            metric_id=metric_id,
            category=DiagnosisCategory.CONFOUND,
            evidence=f"Active confounds: {', '.join(active_confounds)}",
            confidence="low",
            recommended_correction=CourseCorrection.OPTIMISE,
            rationale="External factors may be masking genuine benefit; monitor next quarter",
        )
    return BenefitDiagnosis(
        metric_id=metric_id,
        category=DiagnosisCategory.UNKNOWN,
        evidence="No clear root cause identified",
        confidence="low",
        recommended_correction=CourseCorrection.REMEDIATE,
        rationale="Deeper investigation required",
    )

The GenAI system is working but the benefit gap is not yet closed. Optimise interventions are lightweight and owned by the programme team: improving the model, increasing adoption through training, refining prompts, or adding retrieval-augmented generation to improve accuracy. These do not require sponsor sign-off unless they carry material cost.

A significant gap exists that the programme team cannot close alone. Remediation requires a structured intervention plan co-owned by the business: process redesign, headcount change, system integration work, or supplier engagement. It requires sponsor approval because it involves cost and organisational change.

Some benefits are genuinely unachievable — the underlying assumption was wrong, the market changed, or the technology is not fit for the use case. Retiring a benefit is not a failure; it is disciplined portfolio management. The formal retire process: (1) document the root cause; (2) get sign-off from the business owner and sponsor; (3) record the write-off in the benefits register with the rationale; (4) remove the metric from future tracking. A pivot redirects the investment toward a different benefit that is achievable with the existing system.
The quarterly review meeting runs to a strict agenda. Drift from this structure is the primary reason reviews become unproductive.
Recap: what did we commit to last quarter?
Actuals vs baseline: walk the RAG dashboard
Decide course corrections and assign owners
Confirm next review date and communication plan

Slot 3 is the critical one. Each off-track metric gets 5 minutes: 2 minutes of data presentation, 3 minutes of root-cause discussion. If the root cause is unclear in 3 minutes, the action is to investigate and report back — not to debate longer in the meeting.
Some course corrections exceed the programme team's authority. Define escalation thresholds in the review charter so participants know when they need sponsor input.
: within programme team authority. No escalation needed if cost is within existing budget.
: PM and business owner co-approve. Document in benefits register.
: require sponsor approval and a formal change request to the business case.
: always requires sponsor approval and CFO awareness if the benefit was used to justify the investment case.
Benefit shortfall > 40% of target after 6 months
: mandatory escalation to steering committee for a formal programme health review.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class ReviewEscalation:
    metric_id: str
    trigger: str
    escalation_target: str
    required_output: str
    deadline: date

def check_escalation_triggers(
    outcomes: list[MetricReviewOutcome],
    review_date: date,
    months_since_launch: int,
) -> list[ReviewEscalation]:
    from datetime import timedelta
    escalations = []
    for o in outcomes:
        # Benefit retirement always escalates
        if o.course_correction == CourseCorrection.RETIRE:
            escalations.append(ReviewEscalation(
                metric_id=o.metric_id,
                trigger="Benefit retirement decision",
                escalation_target="Programme Sponsor + CFO",
                required_output="Signed benefit write-off record",
                deadline=review_date + timedelta(days=14),
            ))
        # Major shortfall at 6+ months
        if months_since_launch >= 6 and o.progress_pct < 60 and o.rag == "RED":
            escalations.append(ReviewEscalation(
                metric_id=o.metric_id,
                trigger=f"Progress {o.progress_pct:.0f}% after {months_since_launch} months",
                escalation_target="Steering Committee",
                required_output="Programme health review with go/no-go on continued investment",
                deadline=review_date + timedelta(days=30),
            ))
    return escalations
When a PMO oversees multiple GenAI programmes, individual quarterly reviews should feed a single annual
that aggregates actuals across all programmes, identifies systemic patterns, and makes cross-programme investment recommendations.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class PortfolioBenefitsSnapshot:
    """
    Annual roll-up of benefits actuals across all GenAI programmes.
    """
    year: int
    programmes: list[str]
    total_claimed_benefit_gbp: float      # sum of all business case projections
    total_realised_benefit_gbp: float     # sum of all confirmed actuals
    average_attribution_pct: float        # avg GenAI attribution across metrics
    programmes_on_track: int
    programmes_remediating: int
    programmes_retired: int
    systemic_issues: list[str]            # patterns across programmes (e.g. "low adoption in all customer-facing tools")
    recommended_actions: list[str]

    @property
    def realisation_rate(self) -> float:
        """Ratio of realised to claimed benefit."""
        if not self.total_claimed_benefit_gbp:
            return 0.0
        return self.total_realised_benefit_gbp / self.total_claimed_benefit_gbp

    def health_summary(self) -> str:
        total = len(self.programmes)
        return (
            f"Portfolio {self.year}: {total} programmes | "
            f"£{self.total_realised_benefit_gbp:,.0f} realised of "
            f"£{self.total_claimed_benefit_gbp:,.0f} claimed "
            f"({self.realisation_rate:.0%}) | "
            f"{self.programmes_on_track} on track, "
            f"{self.programmes_remediating} remediating, "
            f"{self.programmes_retired} retired"
        )


def build_portfolio_snapshot(
    reviews: list["BenefitsRealisationReview"],
    year: int,
) -> PortfolioBenefitsSnapshot:
    """
    Aggregate individual programme reviews into a portfolio snapshot.
    Assumes each review carries a 'net_realised_gbp' field (not in the
    dataclass above; add it in production implementations).
    """
    on_track = sum(
        1 for r in reviews
        if all(o.rag == "GREEN" for o in r.metric_outcomes)
    )
    remediating = sum(
        1 for r in reviews
        if any(o.course_correction.value == "remediate" for o in r.metric_outcomes)
    )
    retired = sum(
        1 for r in reviews
        if any(o.course_correction.value == "retire" for o in r.metric_outcomes)
    )
    return PortfolioBenefitsSnapshot(
        year=year,
        programmes=[r.programme for r in reviews],
        total_claimed_benefit_gbp=0.0,   # populated from business cases
        total_realised_benefit_gbp=0.0,  # populated from confirmed actuals
        average_attribution_pct=0.0,
        programmes_on_track=on_track,
        programmes_remediating=remediating,
        programmes_retired=retired,
        systemic_issues=[],
        recommended_actions=[],
    )

The portfolio review is the PMO's opportunity to identify systemic issues — for example, if seven out of ten programmes cite low adoption as the root cause for benefit shortfalls, the issue is not individual programme execution but rather an enterprise-wide adoption enablement gap. That finding should drive a portfolio-level intervention (change management practice, user experience standards, executive mandate for adoption) rather than seven separate remediation plans.
No adoption support; users not incentivised
Enterprise change management practice; usage KPIs in performance reviews
Model quality gaps in customer-facing tools
Insufficient eval rigour at launch gate
Standardise eval requirements; add quality gate to launch checklist
Benefits claimed but not tracked past M+3
Require named benefits owner in every business case; PMO cadence
Standardise assumption validation; independent review for benefits > £500k

Benefits realisation reviews close the accountability loop between the investment case and measured outcomes. The quarterly cycle — Collect, Assess, Diagnose, Decide — produces either a confirmation that benefits are on track or a specific course correction (Optimise, Remediate, or Retire) with a named owner and a deadline. Escalation thresholds defined in the review charter prevent decisions from stalling inside the meeting. Without this discipline, GenAI programme ROI claims remain anecdotal rather than evidenced.
